text: The Stoic concept of oikeiosis — natural affiliation or appropriation — described how a creature naturally draws toward what benefits its own constitution. In infants, this begins as simple self-preservation. But as human rationality develops, oikeiosis expands outward: we come to recognize other rational beings as continuous with ourselves, their flourishing as genuinely connected to ours. Hierocles, a second-century Stoic, described this as a series of concentric circles — self, family, community, humanity — that wisdom progressively contracted. To live naturally, then, was to extend rational care outward, treating the welfare of strangers not as charity but as logic.
question: According to the passage, what did Hierocles' concentric circles represent in the context of oikeiosis?

options.text: The hierarchy of obligations one owes, from self outward to strangers
The stages of rational development that wisdom progressively contracted inward
The different spheres of nature that a Stoic must harmonize simultaneously
Expanding circles of natural affiliation that wisdom drew closer together
